context.description: A philosophical prose page argues that people explain their own choices and beliefs retroactively, just as market commentators explain stock movements after the fact. The passage criticizes retrospective certainty and uses the stock analyst analogy to question claims of knowing the real causes behind decisions and identities.
normalization_notes: Translated German prose into English summary and retrieval text.
No chapter heading visible; classified as philosophical consciousness-related prose.

rows.text_content: Page 16 of the book manuscript; philosophical prose on self-interpretation and retrospective explanation.
rows.paragraph_text: The passage argues that we explain our own choices and beliefs after the fact, in the same way stock commentators explain market moves retroactively.

rows.text_content: Retroactive certainty is criticized as a false understanding of causes.
rows.paragraph_text: A stock market commentator can always explain why a price moved after it has already moved, but that does not mean they can predict the move in advance.

rows.text_content: The analogy is extended to everyday self-explanations.
rows.paragraph_text: The same pattern is applied to personal preferences, career decisions, marriage, brands, philosophy, politics, and ideology, which people explain to themselves only afterward.
